title: Top 100 Things to Do in Burlington
tagline: One hundred experiences, ranked. Argue with us.
intro:
Not a list of addresses — a hundred things to actually do, ranked by how essential each one is to Burlington specifically. An experience you can't have anywhere else beats one that's merely nice, which is why a creemee outranks some very good restaurants. Each entry links to the places involved; tap through for maps, hours, and the full details.

The top ten is the answer to "I have one day, what do I do." The rest is the answer to living here.

items.title: Stroll the Church Street Marketplace
items.body: Start where everyone starts, and don't feel bad about it. Four brick blocks of pedestrian-only downtown — buskers, patios, local shops from legendary gear basements to the state's quirkiest gift store — have been Burlington's living room since 1981. Come back at night when the string lights are on and it's a different place.

items.title: Watch the sun set over Lake Champlain from the Waterfront Park swings
items.body: Charles Dickens allegedly called Champlain's sunsets the best in the world, and the yellow porch swings facing the Adirondacks are the correct venue for judging his claim. Locals check the sky around 7, then walk down. It never gets old, and it's free every single night.

items.title: Bike the Island Line Trail out into the middle of the lake
items.body: Rent a bike at Local Motion and ride a converted rail causeway three miles straight into open water — lake on both sides, mountains on both horizons. In summer, the bike ferry carries you across "the cut" to the Champlain Islands; push on to a vineyard and call it a full day.

items.title: Eat a maple creemee — this is not optional
items.body: Vermont's soft-serve combines the state's two great exports, dairy and maple, and eating one near the water is the single most Vermont thing you can do. The waterfront windows handle the classic experience; the pilgrimage stands (Palmer Lane in Jericho, vegan Offbeat in Winooski) settle the arguments.

items.title: Crawl the Pine Street breweries
items.body: Four-plus taprooms inside half a mile: Zero Gravity's patio, Switchback's unchanged 1996 flagship, Citizen's cider, Queen City's old-world lagers, BBCO's arcade room. Walkable, unpretentious, and the densest evidence for Vermont's most-breweries-per-capita title.

items.title: Drink a Foam beer at the water's edge at golden hour
items.body: World-class IPAs poured a stone's throw from the lake, with Deep City next door handling the food. Time it for sunset and you've stacked two of Burlington's best experiences into one folding chair.

items.title: Spend Saturday morning at the farmers market
items.body: Produce, wood-fired everything, apple cider slushies, and half the city moving at quarter speed. The Old North End's smaller market is the local-secret counterpart, with food that mirrors the most diverse neighborhood in Vermont.

items.title: Lose a full day at Shelburne Museum
items.body: One collector moved thirty-nine buildings — a lighthouse, a round barn, a jail — onto forty-five acres, then parked a full-size 1906 steamboat on the lawn. Folk art, Monets, a working carousel, and the strangest museum campus in America, fifteen minutes south.

items.title: Walk the Vanderbilt estate at Shelburne Farms
items.body: A working farm on a former Vanderbilt-Webb estate with Olmsted-shaped grounds rolling down to the lake. Watch cheddar being made, let kids loose in the farm barn, then get a drink at the Inn as the sun drops behind the Adirondacks.

items.title: Meet the lake's residents at ECHO
items.body: The waterfront science center stocks its tanks from the water outside — Champlain's own sturgeon, turtles, and frogs — plus the full Champ lake-monster treatment. The best rainy-day building in the city, and better on a clear one.

items.title: Swim at the city beaches
items.body: Burlington keeps real sand beaches inside city limits: North Beach for the classic scene (and the tiki bar), Leddy for elbow room, Texaco for the dogs, Oakledge's cove for a quick plunge off the Greenway. Pick by mood; the lake is the same everywhere.

items.title: Ride the Greenway end to end
items.body: Eight flat, car-free miles along the entire city shoreline, connecting every beach, park, and creemee window between Oakledge and the Winooski River. Bikeable in under half an hour, but nobody sensible does it that fast.

items.title: Stand on 500 million years of geology at Rock Point
items.body: A short cedar-shaded walk on quiet church land ends at the Champlain Thrust Fault — ancient rock shoved bodily over younger stone, visible right at the waterline. Geology classes travel from other states for this; you need a free online pass and twenty minutes.

items.title: See a show at the Flynn
items.body: A restored art-deco house on Main Street that pulls national tours a city ten times this size would envy — Broadway runs, jazz legends, and the anchor stage of the Discover Jazz Festival every June.

items.title: Do a full night at Radio Bean and the Lamp Shop
items.body: Start with an early set in the Bean's cluttered, beloved chaos, then slide next door to a room lit entirely by vintage lamps for the late show. This is the eclectic heart of Burlington's music scene, and there's rarely a cover.

items.title: Catch a free show on Church Street
items.body: Live music spills out of Red Square most nights, and the side patio turns summer sets into street parties — usually with no cover. The reliable answer to "is anything actually happening tonight?" downtown.

items.title: Book the mushroom toast at Hen of the Wood
items.body: The benchmark for Vermont farm-to-table, in both Burlington and the original Waterbury grist mill. The mushroom toast has never left the menu because the customers would not allow it.

items.title: Share everything at Honey Road
items.body: Eastern Mediterranean mezze that locals pick for their own celebrations — the fried halloumi has a citywide reputation. Reservations are a sport; the walk-in bar seats are the cheat code.

items.title: Take the easiest great hike in Vermont: Mt. Philo
items.body: Twenty minutes south, a short climb (or a lazy drive up the auto road) buys the best panoramic view of the Champlain Valley, full stop. Philo Ridge Farm across the road handles the post-summit meal.

items.title: Earn Camel's Hump
items.body: Vermont's most iconic profile and its best serious day-hike, 45 minutes out. Go up Forest City and down Burrows for the mossy Brush Brook scenery, and watch for the WW2 bomber crash site off the bad-weather bypass south of the summit.

items.title: Get to the top of Mount Mansfield — any way you like
items.body: The state's highest point offers three routes: the Long Trail on foot, Stowe's gondola, or the auto toll road to the ridge. Ice-age alpine tundra survives along the top, and the Route 108 drive through Smugglers Notch to get there is a destination itself. Underhill State Park is the quiet local approach.

items.title: Get out on the water under sail
items.body: Rent a kayak or paddleboard at the Community Sailing Center, book a BYOB sunset sail on the Whistling Man schooner, or take the Spirit of Ethan Allen's narrated cruise. The city looks better from the lake — everyone should see it from out there once.

items.title: Eat dinner practically in the lake
items.body: Burlington's waterfront dining is a genre: Splash's floating deck at the boathouse, the Spot on the Dock's surf-shack tables, Shanty's fried perch, Skinny Pancake's sunset-odds patio. Afternoon cocktails, water under your feet.

items.title: Laugh at Vermont Comedy Club
items.body: An intimate, highly rated room pulling national names and sharpening local ones, with improv and classes on the side. The reliable date-night closer downtown.

items.title: Catch a touring act at Higher Ground
items.body: The region's essential mid-size venue — two rooms, all genres, plus drag shows and the bands that can't fit anywhere else in Vermont. Check the calendar before any visit; something's on.

items.title: Line up for a proper Burlington breakfast
items.body: Weekend breakfast here means a short wait and no regrets. Handy's is the generations-old Maple Street diner; the Friendly Toast does all-day retro brunch downtown; and Sneakers, across the river in Winooski, has drawn a maple-bacon crowd for years. Any of the three sets up the day right.

items.title: Walk the redstone cliffs at Red Rocks
items.body: Shaded trails along 270-million-year-old quartzite cliffs over the lake, with swimming below in summer. The South End's wildest half-mile, minutes from the breweries.

items.title: Climb the ADA treehouse and find the Earth Clock at Oakledge
items.body: Oakledge packs the world's first fully accessible treehouse, a Stonehenge-style stone circle for sunset, kayak rentals, and a swimming cove into one lakefront park. The all-ages all-abilities crown jewel of the parks system.

items.title: Do the South End Art Hop weekend
items.body: Every September the arts district throws open hundreds of studios for Burlington's biggest art celebration — music, food trucks, and work you can actually afford. The neighborhood's whole identity, distilled into one weekend.

items.title: Wander the Soda Plant halls
items.body: A former ginger-ale factory now packed with working studios, galleries, makers, Brio's roastery, and a cocktail lounge named for the building's old product. Walk in, drift, leave with something unplanned.

items.title: Gallery-hop downtown on a First Friday
items.body: BCA's contemporary shows anchor Church Street's art scene, with Frog Hollow's Vermont craft gallery steps away. Free, air-conditioned culture in the middle of the marketplace stroll.

items.title: Sing with the supporters at a Vermont Green FC match
items.body: Summer soccer at Virtue Field with a supporters' section that outsings most professional clubs, a climate-justice mission in the founding documents, and a 2025 national title to show for it. Games sell out — plan ahead.

items.title: Take in a Lake Monsters game at Centennial Field
items.body: Summer baseball in one of the oldest ballparks in America, with between-innings chaos and Champ himself on the dugout. The cheapest great summer evening in the city.

items.title: Feel Gutterson shake at a UVM hockey game
items.body: Division I hockey in one of college hockey's classic barns — "The Gut" in January is loud, old-school, and the best cheap winter night out Burlington offers.

items.title: Plan a June visit around Discover Jazz
items.body: For one week the whole city becomes a venue — headliners at the Flynn, free stages outdoors, and music leaking out of every bar downtown. Burlington's signature festival.

items.title: Catch the Festival of Fools take over Church Street
items.body: Three days in early August of international-caliber street performers — acrobats, circus acts, comedy — with no tickets and no fences. The crowd and the show share the same bricks.

items.title: Spend Grand Point North weekend on the waterfront
items.body: Grace Potter's own festival brings two nights of serious acts to Waterfront Park every September, with the lake as the backdrop. The most scenic stage in New England.

items.title: Do Waking Windows in Winooski
items.body: Every May the whole of downtown Winooski becomes a festival — stages in bars, on corners, in the mill — built around discovery rather than headliners. The best value in Vermont music.

items.title: Eat from the trucks at the South End Get Down
items.body: Friday nights on Pine Street: a rotating fleet of food trucks, live music, and outdoor drinking in the arts district. Summer's default no-plans plan.

items.title: Golden hour at Summervale
items.body: Thursday evenings at the Intervale — food trucks, local bands, and actual farm fields inside city limits doing the scenery work. Free to walk into, hard to leave.

items.title: Cross the river for a Winooski night out
items.body: The "Brooklyn of Burlington" packs Four Quarters' taproom, Misery Loves Co.'s inventive kitchen, and the Monkey House's proving-ground stage around one walkable circulator. A full evening without moving your car.

items.title: Get a window seat over the Winooski falls
items.body: Waterworks is built into the old Champlain Mill with glass directly over the roaring falls — time it for spring melt. Walk the Riverwalk after to hear what powered a whole mill town.

items.title: Tacos, mezcal, then a creemee at Gordo
items.body: House-nixtamalized tortillas and mezcal on North Winooski Ave, with the Little Gordo creemee window bolted on for summer. The taco-then-creemee one-two exists nowhere else.

items.title: Slurp pho on North Street
items.body: Pho Hong is the broth all local pho is measured against, and Cafe Dim Sum's handmade dumplings are two blocks of the same Old North End food renaissance. Cheap, deep, and beloved.

items.title: Eat your way through the Old North End
items.body: Monday shawarma at the Wise Fool, May Day's small-room cooking, Poppy's counter, Henry Street's sandwiches, Willow's bagels — the ONE is where Burlington's most interesting food quietly lives now. No reservations culture, all neighborhood.

items.title: Bring cash to Four Corners of the Earth
items.body: A Pine Street deli of wildly original sandwiches that read like they shouldn't work and do — the lamb is the perennial answer. Cash only, worth the ATM trip.

items.title: Taste the Montreal-vs-New York bagel debate at Myer's
items.body: Wood-fired, honey-boiled, Mile End-style bagels on Pine Street — smaller, denser, sweeter, and the closest thing to Montreal south of the border. Mornings only if you want the full choice.

items.title: Get fries and a creemee at Al's, unchanged since 1948
items.body: Fresh-cut fries from a South Burlington institution whose formula hasn't needed revision in 76 years. Order at the counter, eat in the car, feel continuity with generations of Vermonters.

items.title: Have a grown-up brunch off Church Street
items.body: Not every Burlington brunch is a diner. The Grey Jay does a polished, cocktail-forward take a block off the marketplace — the move when you want brunch to feel like an occasion rather than a recovery.

items.title: Have the best-pizza-downtown argument properly
items.body: Verità's blistered Neapolitan margherita versus American Flatbread's blazing open hearth (where Zero Gravity was born). Research both sides. Repeat as needed.

items.title: Catch Duff Hour at Three Needs
items.body: The famously cheap early-evening pint window at this brewpub-dive hybrid is a Burlington rite of passage — possibly the last true beer deal downtown.

items.title: Find the door to Lincoln's
items.body: Every drink is five dollars, it's cash only, and the basement entrance is deliberately unmarked. A speakeasy that runs on word of mouth; consider this your word.

items.title: Live jazz and a pastrami at Deli 126
items.body: One part New York deli, one part 1920s cocktail lounge, with live jazz regularly and lighting that flatters everyone. The most transporting room downtown.

items.title: Play the vintage cabinets at the Archives
items.body: A dim bar packed with restored arcade machines and pinball — craft beer in one hand, high score in the other. Nostalgia, correctly executed.

items.title: Discover the Pinball Co-op
items.body: Roughly forty pristine machines, one flat fee, unlimited play, Wednesday and Friday nights — a volunteer-run, alcohol-free co-op where every machine works because the players maintain them. One of the county's best-kept secrets.

items.title: Climb the walls at Petra Cliffs
items.body: A friendly indoor climbing gym that takes true beginners and humbles experts, with youth programs that half the county's kids pass through. Winter's best workout.

items.title: Skate the park Tony Hawk endorsed
items.body: A-Dog Williams Skatepark sits right on the lake with downtown views — a world-class concrete park named for a beloved local DJ, endorsed by the man himself.

items.title: Double feature at the Sunset Drive-In
items.body: One of New England's last drive-ins, up in Colchester — pack the car, tune the radio, and do summer the way it used to work.

items.title: See a movie for free (or a mystery one)
items.body: Main Street Landing screens classics free of admission; VTIFF runs international films monthly; and Required Viewing shows a surprise film from a three-word clue, twice a month, also free. Burlington's film scene is a genuine bargain.

items.title: Order the mock eel at A Single Pebble
items.body: Chinese banquet dining in a Bank Street rowhouse where the crispy shiitake "eel" has been converting skeptics on faith for twenty years. Bring a full table and share everything.

items.title: Do a proper sit-down night on Church Street
items.body: Leunig's bistro corner has been the see-and-be-seen classic for decades; the Farmhouse's beer list and winter basement fireplace make it the year-round fallback that never misses.

items.title: Warm up over a bowl of ramen
items.body: When Vermont turns gray, Gaku's rich tonkotsu is the most restorative seat downtown — the January line out the door makes the case better than we can. The meal that turns a cold night around.

items.title: Join the 3 AM kabob line
items.body: Ahli Baba's and Kountry Kart have absorbed decades of Burlington's closing-time hunger. Nothing about either has changed in years, which is precisely the appeal.

items.title: Have a real diner morning
items.body: Henry's has held down Bank Street since the 1920s; the Parkway is an authentic 1950s stainless car on Williston Road. Big breakfasts, bottomless coffee, zero irony.

items.title: Do the coffee crawl
items.body: Muddy Waters' wooden cave for reading, August First's famously screen-free tables for actual conversation, Onyx Tonics for the technicians, Speeder & Earl's and Scout for the neighborhoods. Burlington takes its caffeine seriously and its laptops optional.

items.title: Climb Ethan Allen Tower
items.body: A short New North End walk up to a 1905 stone tower with sweeping views of city, lake, and mountains — the best effort-to-view ratio in Burlington.

items.title: Tour the Revolutionary hero's homestead
items.body: Ethan Allen's 1785 farmhouse in the Intervale, with a recreated Abenaki village and daily tours in season — Vermont's founding myth, at the source.

items.title: Walk the Intervale's farm trails
items.body: Hundreds of acres of working farms and river trails inside city limits — flat, scenic, and startling every time you remember you're ten minutes from Church Street. Cross-country ski it in winter.

items.title: Take the sunset walk nobody knows about
items.body: Lakeview Cemetery's paths run along the lake bluffs through Victorian monuments — quiet, beautiful, and functioning exactly as 19th-century garden cemeteries intended: as public green space.

items.title: Spend an hour at the Fleming
items.body: UVM's art museum holds Vermont's most comprehensive collection — American landscapes, global anthropology, and a Rockwell — in a building you'll have nearly to yourself on weekdays.

items.title: Read something in the Carnegie reading room
items.body: Fletcher Free is an original Carnegie library still doing its everyday job just off Church Street. The restored reading room is the best free interior downtown.

items.title: Do the bookstore crawl
items.body: Phoenix for new and local authors, Crow's creaky-floored used stacks a block away, Speaking Volumes on Pine for books-plus-vinyl-plus-old-maps. Downtown supports three real bookstores; reward it.

items.title: Go on a vintage safari
items.body: Barge Canal's multi-dealer mid-century trove on Pine, Junktiques' dig-for-it Old North End racks, and Five Corners' massive Essex depot. Budget double the time you planned; you'll use it.

items.title: Flip through the record stores
items.body: Burlington Records downtown and Autumn Records in Winooski — one deep and classic, one sharp and curated by a working experimental musician. Vinyl culture is alive here.

items.title: Build a waterfront picnic from City Market
items.body: The downtown co-op's shelves are a tour of Vermont producers — cheese, bread, beer, Ms. Weinerz donuts when they land. Assemble, walk four blocks west, eat at the water.

items.title: Watch glass being blown at AO
items.body: A working South End glass studio with free Monday–Saturday viewing and hands-on classes where you shape your own piece. Mesmerizing in exactly the way screens aren't.

items.title: Tour the Teddy Bear Hospital
items.body: The Vermont Teddy Bear factory tour in Shelburne shows the production floor and the "hospital" that repairs any bear under a lifetime guarantee — genuinely more charming than it has any right to be.

items.title: Taste cold-climate wine and wild cider
items.body: Shelburne Vineyard pours Vermont-grown wines minutes from the museum; Shacksbury in Vergennes ferments apples foraged from old hedgerows into something closer to wine than soda. The state's other fermentation scene.

items.title: Make the pilgrimage to the Alchemist
items.body: Heady Topper started the haze craze that conquered American beer, and the source is 40 minutes away in Stowe. Tastes at the visitor center, cans to go, bragging rights forever.

items.title: Run the Route 100 gauntlet: cider donuts, Heady, and the Flavor Graveyard
items.body: The Waterbury corridor stacks Cold Hollow's warm-from-the-fryer cider donuts, Prohibition Pig's smokehouse-brewery lunch, and the Ben & Jerry's factory tour with its deceased-flavor cemetery. One road, one afternoon, no regrets.

items.title: Drive to the best brewery on Earth
items.body: Hill Farmstead, on a Greensboro hilltop over an hour out, keeps winning "world's best brewery" from the international rating sites. It's a genuine pilgrimage — check hours first, bring a cooler, and make a Northeast Kingdom day of it.

items.title: Drink a lager where the Sound of Music family landed
items.body: The von Trapps settled in Stowe after the story everyone knows, and their lodge now brews proper Austrian lagers served in a timber bierhall with mountain views. Cross-country ski trails in winter complete the alpine cosplay.

items.title: Jump into the Bolton Potholes
items.body: A staircase of natural stone bowls on Joiner Brook, 25 minutes out — the classic Vermont swimming hole. Weekdays or early; parking is tight and the locals know.

items.title: Swim behind the curtain at Bristol Falls
items.body: A broad waterfall on the New Haven River with a deep pool below — on the right day you can swim behind the falling water itself. Forty minutes south, permanent memory.

items.title: Float a lazy river in a tube
items.body: Umiak's Waterbury and Richmond outposts rent tubes and run the shuttle; the river does everything else. The correct answer to any 90-degree afternoon.

items.title: Paddle out from the Surf Club
items.body: SUP and windsurf rentals plus a beach-club scene at the bottom of Lakeside Ave — Burlington's closest thing to surf culture, and it commits to the bit.

items.title: Drop in on a dragon boat Saturday
items.body: The Malia club paddles twenty-person dragon boats out of the sailing center and welcomes drop-ins on Saturday mornings, gear and instruction included. Yes, really — you can just show up.

items.title: Sweat, then jump in the lake at Savu
items.body: Private wood-fired saunas by the water near Hula, with cold plunges between rounds — and in deep winter, a hole in an actual frozen lake. The Essex Resort's spa is the plush, full-day alternative.

items.title: Take small kids to Sand Bar
items.body: The natural sandbar keeps open Lake Champlain waist-deep for a long way out — the calmest, warmest family swimming in the county, twenty minutes north.

items.title: Find your quiet trail
items.body: Centennial Woods' urban forest, the undeveloped loop at Colchester Pond, Sunny Hollow's volunteer-groomed pines, Niquette Bay's lake-shore paths, or Audubon's beaver ponds in Huntington. Within thirty minutes there is always somewhere with no crowd.

items.title: Ski Bolton under the lights
items.body: Leave work, be on a lit trail by six — Bolton Valley's night skiing is the locals' winter cheat code, with Catamount's groomed fat-bike and snowshoe loops in Williston as the flatland version.

items.title: Walk on the lake to the sea caves
items.body: When a long hard freeze locks the ice, locals walk out to the Donahue Sea Caves — a destination that exists only a few weeks a year. Wait for sustained deep cold, never go early-season, never go alone.

items.title: Witness the lit-up cement truck parade
items.body: Every St. Patrick's Day, SD Ireland covers its concrete mixer fleet in thousands of lights and parades it through town. It is absurd. It is beloved. It exists nowhere else on Earth.

items.title: Run — or relay — the Vermont City Marathon
items.body: Memorial Day weekend's citywide event lets normal humans split the 26.2 as a relay while whole neighborhoods turn out with hoses and cowbells. Finish line: the waterfront, obviously.

items.title: Do a full night at the Champlain Valley Fair
items.body: Late August in Essex Junction: midway rides, big-name grandstand concerts, maple everything, and the demolition derby — pure, chaotic Vermont summer's-end tradition since 1922.

items.title: Ride the dinner train down the valley
items.body: White tablecloths aboard vintage rail cars rolling along the lake and through the Champlain Valley — the only dining room in Vermont with a new view every ten minutes.

items.title: Take the ghost walk
items.body: Queen City Ghostwalk's evening tours are real local history dressed as ghost stories — fires, hauntings, and everything the plaques leave out. Book October early.

items.title: Hunt the niche museums
items.body: Hand-carved birds in Huntington, the world's first snowflake photographs in Jericho, free shipwreck archaeology in Ferrisburgh, a documented Underground Railroad farmstead, a 1783 log cabin on the islands. Vermont's small museums are deeply, wonderfully specific.

items.title: See small professional theater done right
items.body: Vermont Stage's contemporary work at Main Street Landing in season, and Saint Michael's Playhouse's Equity summer stock in Colchester — rooms small enough to watch the actors think.

items.title: Pay your respects to the World's Tallest Filing Cabinet
items.body: Finish the list at Burlington's monument to bureaucratic absurdity — a tower of filing cabinets reaching for the clouds off Flynn Ave, complete with local lore about treasure in the top drawer. Entirely underwhelming, completely mandatory. Collect the whole quirky-landmark set while you're at it.

title: The Perfect Burlington Saturday
tagline: One ideal day in the city, hour by hour.
intro:
This is the sequence that shows Burlington at its best — market, water, South End, dinner, a show. It's built for a warm-season Saturday; in winter, skip the causeway ride and the waterfront lunch, and lean on the South End taprooms, the Fleming, and a fireside dinner instead.

Everything here links to the full details. Adjust freely — the point is the shape of the day, not the exact clock.

items.title: Breakfast worth the small wait
items.body: Start slow. Handy's has fed Burlington from the same Maple Street counter for generations, and the Friendly Toast does all-day retro brunch a block off Church Street. A short line at either is a good sign, not a deterrent.

items.title: Graze the farmers market
items.body: Saturday mornings the market fills with Vermont produce, wood-fired everything, and cider slushies. Eat your way down one side and don't plan lunch too early — half the city is moving at quarter speed and you should too.

items.title: Take the waterfront while the light's good
items.body: Walk down to the yellow porch swings facing the Adirondacks. This is the postcard, and it's free every day of the year. Sit for longer than you planned.

items.title: Bike straight out onto the lake
items.body: Rent from Local Motion at the boathouse and ride the Greenway onto the Island Line causeway — a rail bed three miles into open water, lake on both sides. Turn around whenever; in summer the bike ferry carries you across the cut to the islands.

items.title: Lunch with your feet near the water
items.body: Back at the harbor, eat where the lake is the view — Skinny Pancake's crepes and sunset-odds patio, or Splash's floating deck if you want a drink with it.

items.title: Pine Street: beer, art, and molten glass
items.body: Trade the water for the South End. Zero Gravity's patio and Switchback's unchanged flagship are two blocks apart, the Soda Plant's studios and roastery are a short walk, and AO Glass has artisans working if you time it right. Pace yourself; dinner's coming.

items.title: Dinner you'll bring up for months
items.body: Book ahead. Honey Road's mezze and Hen of the Wood's mushroom toast are the two tables people plan trips around; Leunig's bistro corner is the see-and-be-seen classic if you want Church Street energy.

items.title: A show, then find the unmarked door
items.body: Catch stand-up at the Vermont Comedy Club or whatever's loud at Red Square, which runs live music nearly every night. Close it out at Lincoln's, the five-dollar-everything speakeasy whose entrance you have to find yourself. Now you know it's there.

title: A Perfect Day on the Water
tagline: Sunrise bagel to sunset sail — the summer lake day.
intro: Burlington's whole reason for being is the lake, and on the right summer day you can build an entire itinerary that never leaves its edge. This one runs paddle to beach to causeway to sunset sail. Sunscreen is the only hard requirement.

items.title: Coffee and a bagel to carry
items.body: Fuel first. Myer's wood-fired Montreal-style bagels are the local pick, and Scout pairs serious coffee with house-made ice cream for later ideas. Get it to go — the water's waiting.

items.title: Paddle out before the wind picks up
items.body: The Community Sailing Center rents kayaks and paddleboards right on the harbor — mornings are glassy and calm, the best window to get out on Champlain under your own power. No boat of your own required.

items.title: Ride the causeway to the islands
items.body: Grab a bike from Local Motion and ride the Island Line straight into the lake. In season the bike ferry drops you in South Hero — pedal on to Snow Farm Vineyard for a mid-ride glass and, some afternoons, live music on the lawn.

items.title: Claim a patch of beach
items.body: Burlington keeps real sand inside the city. North Beach has the scene and a tiki bar, Oakledge has the swimming cove and the accessible treehouse, Leddy has the elbow room. Pick by mood; the water's the same.

items.title: A creemee by the lake, non-negotiable
items.body: Vermont's maple soft-serve, eaten within sight of the water, is the whole point of summer here. The waterfront windows handle the classic; walk it off along the boardwalk.

items.title: Sunset — under sail or over a Foam beer
items.body: Two great ways to take the golden hour: a BYOB sunset sail on the Whistling Man schooner, or a world-class IPA on Foam's lakeside patio as the sky lights up behind the Adirondacks.

items.title: Dinner on the dock
items.body: End where you spent the day. Splash floats on the harbor, the Spot on the Dock does surf-shack tables at the water's edge, and Shanty's fried perch is the old-school move. Sunset seats go first.

title: Burlington in the Snow
tagline: How locals actually love a Vermont winter day.
intro:
Winter is not the off-season here — it's just a different set of moves. This day trades the beach for groomed trails, a warm bowl, a museum, and the hot-cold ritual that makes February bearable. Dress in layers and lean in.

One honest caution: the sea-caves stop only exists after a long, hard freeze. Never walk out on early-season ice, and never go alone.

items.title: A slow coffee in the warmest room in town
items.body: Ease in at Muddy Waters — all wood, plants, and low light, the cabin-like café built for exactly this kind of gray morning. Read something. There's no rush.

items.title: Get outside anyway
items.body: The trick to Vermont winter is going out into it. Catamount grooms fat-bike and snowshoe loops fifteen minutes away, Bolton Valley is the locals' ski hill (with night skiing later), and the Intervale's flat farm trails take cross-country skis right inside the city.

items.title: Warm up around a bowl of ramen
items.body: Come in from the cold to Gaku's rich tonkotsu — the single most restorative lunch in the city when it's ten degrees out. The January line out the door makes the case better than we can.

items.title: An afternoon that isn't cold
items.body: Bank some indoor hours. The Fleming's collection is nearly yours alone on a weekday, and ECHO's lake tanks are a reliable win with kids when the waterfront's a frozen wind tunnel.

items.title: Walk out to the sea caves — if the lake allows
items.body: In a deep-freeze winter, locals walk across solid ice to the Donahue Sea Caves near the river delta — a destination that only exists a few weeks a year. Only attempt this after sustained hard cold, with company, never early season. When conditions are right, there's nothing else like it.

items.title: Sauna, then the plunge
items.body: The Nordic answer to a cold day: book a private wood-fired sauna at Savu by the lake, cycle hot to cold, and — if you've earned your nerve — drop through a hole in the frozen lake itself.

items.title: Fireside pint, then the Gut
items.body: Thaw out by the Farmhouse's basement fireplace with a Vermont beer, then head up to Gutterson for UVM hockey — one of college hockey's loudest old barns, and the best cheap winter night in town.

title: Free Burlington
tagline: Everything worth doing that costs nothing.
intro: Burlington punches above its size on free things to do — parks, public art, beach access, live music, and galleries that don't charge admission. This list updates automatically from the master list whenever we verify a free entry.

title: Cheap Eats & Drinks
tagline: A $15 budget gets you farther than you think in Vermont.
intro:
Burlington's food scene skews toward quality over price, but there are enough places where you can eat and drink well without watching the tab. Single-dollar sign means a good meal under $20; most are under $15.

The best value in the city is almost always a pint and something at one of the South End taprooms — Zero Gravity's Clean State pilsner and Citizen Cider's Wit's Up are both under $8 and better than anything you'd pay three times as much for somewhere else.

title: Rainy Day Burlington
tagline: Vermont weather is variable. Here's the plan B.
intro: Burlington averages 155 cloudy days per year. The upside: more excuses to visit the Fleming, nurse a beer at a South End taproom, or finally make it to ECHO. This list covers everything indoor-friendly that's worth your time when the Adirondacks disappear into the clouds.

title: Date Night in Burlington
tagline: From a first dinner to the kind of evening you remember.
intro:
Burlington is easy to be romantic in — the lake does a lot of the work. These picks range from a Skinny Pancake patio lunch with good sunset odds, to Leunig's bistro for something properly sit-down, to a Vermont Comedy Club show that breaks the ice without needing you to.

For a full evening: start at the waterfront for drinks, walk down to dinner on Church Street, then catch whatever's at the Flynn or Vermont Comedy Club. The porch swings at Waterfront Park are a reliable last stop.

title: Outdoors & Scenic Burlington
tagline: The lake, the mountains, the trails. All of it.
intro:
Burlington's outdoor assets are the reason people move here and don't leave. The Greenway connects eight miles of lakefront. The Causeway extends into Lake Champlain. Red Rocks has geological formations 270 million years old. And Camel's Hump is 45 minutes away when you need something that feels properly remote.

For a single best outdoor day: Greenway ride south to Oakledge, swim at the Cove, then north to the Skinny Pancake patio. Sunset on the waterfront swings to close.

title: Best for Families & Kids
tagline: Burlington is surprisingly good for families. Here's proof.
intro:
Burlington's kid-friendly options are more interesting than the usual aquarium-and-playground circuit. ECHO has live Champlain aquatic tanks. Oakledge has the world's first fully ADA-accessible treehouse. North Beach has lifeguards. The farmers market is a morning well spent with anyone who can eat food.

Older kids: Petra Cliffs for climbing, Ben & Jerry's for the Flavor Graveyard, Vermont Teddy Bear Factory for the Bear Hospital (genuinely more interesting than it sounds).

title: Burlington's Brewing Scene
tagline: Vermont has more craft breweries per capita than any state. Here's where to start.
intro:
Vermont's craft brewing scene is the densest in the country by per-capita count, and Burlington is the center of it. The South End alone has Zero Gravity, Switchback, Burlington Beer Company, and Citizen Cider within a few blocks of each other.

For a walkable afternoon: start at Zero Gravity for the pilsner, walk two blocks to Switchback for the flagship, then Citizen Cider for a break from hops. Burlington Beer Company has the widest tap list if you want options. Foam is ten minutes north on the waterfront for the sunset.

For something farther out: Fiddlehead in Shelburne is worth the drive if you catch a Thursday release.

title: The Burlington Waterfront
tagline: Fourteen miles of Lake Champlain shoreline, all the best of it.
intro:
Burlington's waterfront is the city's best feature by a wide margin, and it's better when you know where to look. The porch swings at Waterfront Park get the afternoon light. Perkins Pier is where the Spirit of Ethan Allen departs. The dog beach at Texaco Point is a social event most afternoons in summer.

The Greenway connects it all without a car — eight miles from Oakledge in the south to North Beach. Rent a bike from Local Motion at the boathouse if you don't have one.

title: Live Music in Burlington
tagline: From the birthplace of Phish to Grace Potter's own festival.
intro:
Burlington's music scene is disproportionately good for a city of 45,000. Nectar's is where Phish played their first shows. Radio Bean and Light Club Lamp Shop run eclectic programming every evening. Higher Ground brings touring acts that can't fit a full Vermont run anywhere else.

For a free evening: Radio Bean from 5 PM, stay for whatever's on after 8. For a real show: Higher Ground's Showcase Lounge has something most weeknights for under $20.

title: ECHO: The Museum That Lives in the Lake
tagline: Champlain's own frogs, turtles, and monster lore, on the waterfront.
intro: Most aquariums fly their fish in. ECHO's tanks are stocked from the water outside its windows — lake sturgeon, snapping turtles, the frogs you hear at the Intervale in June. That makes it less flashy than a big-city aquarium and much more interesting: you walk out and the exhibit keeps going to the horizon.

sections.heading: What to see
sections.body: The Lake Champlain species tanks are the core — give the sturgeon time, they're older and stranger than they look. Kids gravitate to the touch tank and the water-table floor. And yes, there's Champ: the lake-monster lore gets a real exhibit treatment, sightings map included.

sections.heading: When to go
sections.body: Rainy days are peak crowding for obvious reasons — arrive at opening if the forecast is bad. On a nice day it's the opposite: quiet inside, and you can pair it with the boardwalk and Waterfront Park swings right outside.

sections.heading: Good to know
sections.body: Admission runs about $17 for adults, $13 for kids (verify current prices on their site). Plan on two hours; under-tens can happily go longer. It sits at the bottom of College Street, an easy walk from Church Street.

title: Shelburne Museum: 39 Buildings and a Beached Steamboat
tagline: One collector's magnificent obsession, seven miles south of town.
intro: Electra Havemeyer Webb didn't collect paintings so much as she collected buildings — a lighthouse, a covered bridge, a round barn, a jail — and had them moved to 45 acres in Shelburne starting in 1947. The result is less a museum than a village assembled by one person's taste, and it's unlike anywhere else in New England.

sections.heading: The Ticonderoga
sections.body: The centerpiece: a full-scale 1906 side-wheel steamboat, hauled two miles overland from Lake Champlain in 1955 and permanently installed on the lawn. It's a National Historic Landmark you can walk through deck by deck, and it alone justifies the trip.

sections.heading: How to do it
sections.body: This is a half-day minimum — the grounds are genuinely large. Prioritize the Ticonderoga, the Round Barn, and the Circus Building's 500-foot miniature parade. The museum is seasonal (roughly May through October for the full campus), so check hours before driving down; admission is around $25 for adults.

sections.heading: Getting there
sections.body: Fifteen minutes south of downtown Burlington on Route 7. Pair it with Shelburne Farms or a Fiddlehead stop on the way back and you've built a full day out of one road.

title: Burlington With Your Dog
tagline: A full day out for pets and their people.
intro:
Burlington is an easy city to share with a dog — a beach built for them, a bike path that runs the whole waterfront, taprooms that keep water bowls by the door, and a downtown where most Church Street shops will wave you both in. This itinerary strings the best of it into one day; every stop links to the full details.

Adapted from the "Guide to Pet-Friendly Burlington" by Houndstooth (126 College Street), the downtown pet shop that literally wrote the pamphlet on it. Two ground rules: patios are seasonal, so the outdoor-only spots below are warm-weather plays, and leashes are required most places that aren't a designated dog park or dog beach.

items.title: Coffee where the dog is welcome inside
items.body: A handful of downtown cafés go beyond patio tolerance and actually welcome dogs indoors — Onyx Tonics on College Street (next door to Houndstooth), Kestrel Coffee on Pine or Maple, and Happy Place Cafe on Main. Start slow; the dog park isn't going anywhere.

items.title: Morning romp at the dog beach
items.body: Texaco Beach, tucked between North Beach and the Urban Reserve, is Burlington's designated dog beach — off-leash swimming, lake smells, total joy. If your dog prefers turf to water, the Starr Farm and Urban Reserve dog parks are both in the New North End, with Wheeler (South Burlington) and West Allen (Winooski) close behind.

items.title: Walk the waterfront on the bike path
items.body: The Burlington Bike Path runs eight miles along the lake and is the classic leash-walk in town. Head south from the dog beach toward Waterfront Park and the boathouse, with the Adirondacks doing their thing across the water the whole way.

items.title: Patio lunch by the water
items.body: In patio season, the waterfront eats are all dog-friendly: Skinny Pancake and Splash at the Boathouse right on the water, August First a couple blocks up, Burlington Bay for the creemee window. Order at the counter, claim a table in the shade, share nothing.

items.title: Shop Church Street with the dog
items.body: Most Church Street Marketplace businesses welcome leashed dogs, including Homeport, Kiss the Cook, and Outdoor Gear Exchange. Then swing down College Street: Common Deer, Kylee Boutique, Home & Garden Vermont, Liebling — and Houndstooth itself at 126 College, where the treat gets picked out in person.

items.title: A proper trail before dinner
items.body: Red Rocks and Oakledge are the in-town classics — real woods, lake overlooks, twenty minutes from downtown. With more time and a fresher dog, the regional favorites are Mt. Philo, Shelburne Bay Park, Colchester Pond, the Green Mountain Audubon Center in Huntington, or Camel's Hump if everyone's feeling ambitious.

items.title: Taproom hour
items.body: Burlington's breweries are the most reliably dog-friendly rooms in the city. Switchback and Zero Gravity anchor the South End, Foam has the waterfront patio, and Four Quarters (Winooski), Queen City, Vermont Pub & Brewery, and Citizen Cider round out the circuit. Water bowls are standard equipment.

items.title: If something goes wrong
items.body: Two 24/7 emergency vets serve the area: Burlington Emergency & Veterinary Specialists (BEVS) at 1417 Marshall Avenue, Williston, (802) 863-2387, and Veg ER for Pets at 225 Dorset Street, South Burlington, (802) 225-4032. For urgent-but-not-emergency care: Peak Veterinary Urgent Care in Williston, (802) 878-2022, or Dorset Street Animal Hospital in South Burlington, (802) 221-3090.
